headline: Malaysia FIRST BASELINE CONFIRM at 46.9 / Functional — HRW May 6 submission to UN Committee on the Rights of the Child is in-window institutional evidence: abuses against migrant, refugee, and stateless children (54,000+ UNHCR-registered children among 190,000+ refugees with no legal status; barred from government schools; cannot work legally), SOGI discrimination, immigration enforcement that does not differentiate adults from children. HRW May 4 separate submission flagged new refugee registration system privacy/rights concerns. HRW World Report 2026 documents broad laws limiting freedom of expression, restrictions on peaceful protest, harassment of activists, anti-corruption activism suppressed (three arrests April 2026). Amnesty 'human rights hypocrisy' report April 2026. Published 46.9 baseline is plausible — partial rights protections (active civil society, regional engagement) with structural enforcement gaps. Clean reconstruction.
key_evidence: May 6, 2026 (in-window): HRW submission to UN Committee on the Rights of the Child: abuses against migrant, refugee, and stateless children; SOGI discrimination against children.

HRW data: 190,000+ refugees and asylum seekers registered with UNHCR in Malaysia, including ~54,000 children. UNHCR recognition does not confer legal status under Malaysian law — refugees barred from government schools and cannot work legally.
HRW data: Many children effectively stateless in Sabah and Sarawak; education and basic services barriers documented.
May 4, 2026 (in-window): HRW separate submission — new refugee registration system raises privacy and rights concerns.

Malaysia's Immigration Act 1959/63: does not differentiate refugees, asylum seekers, trafficking victims, undocumented migrants, or adults vs. children — all treated as 'illegal immigrants' (PATI) subject to arrest, detention, deportation.

HRW World Report 2026: broad laws limiting freedom of expression used against critics; restrictions on peaceful protest; harassment of activists. Anti-corruption activism suppressed (three arrests April 2026).
Amnesty International April 2026 annual report: cited Malaysia's 'human rights hypocrisy.'

January 2026 (pre-window context): CRC experts commended Malaysia on maternal/infant health progress while raising concerns on penalties for children in serious crimes and alternatives to institutionalization.

evidence.source: HRW — Malaysia: Submission to the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child
evidence.finding: In-window institutional submission documenting child-rights abuses against migrant, refugee, stateless populations and SOGI discrimination.

evidence.source: HRW — Malaysia New Refugee Registration System Raises Concerns
evidence.finding: Privacy and rights concerns over new refugee registration system; in-window structural concern documented.
Expression restrictions; protest suppression; activist harassment; anti-corruption activist arrests April 2026.

evidence.source: Rakyat Post — Amnesty calls out Malaysia's human rights hypocrisy in annual global report
evidence.finding: Amnesty International April 2026 annual report cited 'human rights hypocrisy' — declared-values vs. enforcement gap.

evidence.source: OHCHR — CRC experts commend Malaysia on maternal/infant health
evidence.finding: Pre-window CRC context: progress on maternal/infant health; concerns on juvenile justice and institutionalization alternatives.

evidence.source: Online Citizen — Malaysia's new refugee registration system raises privacy and rights concerns
evidence.finding: Multi-source confirmation of refugee-system concerns; regional media coverage.

scoring_rationale.AWR: 3.0 sustained at published. A1 detection: CRC engagement, HRW reporting, Amnesty engagement = external monitoring channels active; A4 amplification: media coverage of refugee/expression issues. Anchor 3 sustained.
scoring_rationale.EMP: 3.0 sustained at published. E1 affective resonance: maternal/infant health progress commended by CRC; E2 protected scope: refugee/stateless children excluded from formal protections caps anchor 3. Anchor 3 sustained.
scoring_rationale.ACT: 3.0 sustained at published. AC1 responsiveness: state engages with UN treaty bodies; AC4 resource mobilization: maternal/infant health program operational; refugee/stateless implementation gap caps anchor 3. Anchor 3 sustained.
scoring_rationale.EQU: 2.5 sustained at published. EQ1 universality: refugees, asylum seekers, trafficking victims, stateless persons all treated as 'illegal immigrants' under Immigration Act — protected-scope narrowed; EQ2 categories: SOGI discrimination documented; EQ3 bias awareness: HRW submissions raise structural concerns. Anchor 2-3 boundary at 2.5.
scoring_rationale.BND: 3.0 sustained at published. B5 multilateral engagement: CRC and other UN treaty body engagement sustained; B3 scope clarity: regional ASEAN engagement active. Anchor 3 sustained.
scoring_rationale.ACC: 3.0 sustained at published. AB4 accountability-to-external-norms: engages with HRW, Amnesty, UN CRC, OHCHR; AB1 harm-acknowledgment: maternal/infant health acknowledgment via CRC; expression restrictions cap upward movement. Anchor 3 sustained.
scoring_rationale.SYS: 3.0 sustained at published. S2 long-term-impact: regulatory framework operational; S4 structural critique: HRW and Amnesty reporting provide structural-reform reference; refugee registration system rights concerns are SYS-relevant. Anchor 3 sustained.
scoring_rationale.INT: 2.5 sustained at published. I1 consistency: Amnesty 'hypocrisy' framing reflects declared-values vs. enforcement gap; I2 non-performance: ratifications without operational implementation noted; below anchor 3 reflects sustained values-action gap.

math_check.validator_check: Reconstructs cleanly to 46.9 = published. Sub-threshold drift (0.0). Functional band sustained. Not a boundary case.

negativeSignals: 190,000+ refugees with no legal status under Malaysian law; ~54,000 refugee children barred from government schools; Immigration Act treats all undocumented persons (including children) uniformly; new refugee registration system rights/privacy concerns; SOGI discrimination against children; expression restrictions; peaceful protest restrictions; anti-corruption activists arrested April 2026; Amnesty 'human rights hypocrisy' framing.
positiveSignals: Active engagement with UN treaty bodies (CRC submission cycle); maternal/infant health progress commended; civil society engagement via HRW/Amnesty submissions; ASEAN regional engagement; UNHCR registration system operational (54,000+ children registered despite no legal status).
sector_context: Southeast Asia / ASEAN cluster. Malaysia's 46.9 (Functional) baseline is consistent with the mixed evidence pattern: active multilateral engagement and partial rights protections offset by structural enforcement gaps on refugees, expression, and SOGI. Lower INT (2.5 vs 3.0 default) reflects the documented values-action gap.
open_actions_resolved: First baseline established with HRW CRC submission as in-window institutional anchor
Anti-corruption activist arrests (April 2026) noted as INT concern
open_actions_carried: Watch: CRC review outcome and Malaysia's response
Watch: refugee registration system implementation and rights safeguards
Watch: SOGI policy and any rights-protective amendments
Watch: expression and protest restrictions evolution
Watch: anti-corruption enforcement (consistent application or selective)
